Safactory: A Scalable Agentic Model Factory for
Trustworthy Autonomous Intelligence
Shanghai AI Laboratory∗
Abstract
As large models evolve from conversational assistants into autonomous agents,
challenges increasingly arise from long-horizon decision making, tool use, and real
environment interaction.
Existing agenticinfrastructure remain fragmented across
evaluation, data management, and agent evolution, making it difficult to discover risks
systematically and improve models in a continuous closed loop. In this report, we
present Safactory, a scalable agent factory for trustworthy autonomous intelligence.
Safactory integrates three tightly coupled platforms: a Parallel Simulation Platform
for trajectory generation, a Trustworthy Data Platform for trajectory storage and
experience extraction, and an Autonomous Evolution Platform for asynchronous
reinforcement learning and on-policy distillation. As far as we know, Safactory is the first
framework to propose a unified evolutionary pipeline for next-generation trustworthy
autonomous intelligence.

Safactory: A Scalable Agent Factory for Trustworthy Autonomous Intelligence
Introduction
1.1
A Panoramic View of AI Agent Safety Risks
With the rapid development of applications such as companion AI, DeepResearch, intelligent
terminals, and embodied AI, large language models are moving from "conversational assistants"
to "autonomous agents." These systems are not only responsible for answering questions but
also continuously perceive their environment, plan tasks, invoke tools, and directly influence
external systems.
This brings stronger application capabilities, but also imposes higher safety requirements.
Unlike traditional single-turn models, the risks of agents are not limited to harmful outputs;
they accumulate progressively throughout the process of "perception →decision-making →
execution →feedback," ultimately manifesting as real-world environmental consequences.
As shown in Figure 2, violations, jailbreak inductions, and explicit attacks are merely the
risks above the surface. Beneath the waterline lie more insidious issues such as tool misuse,
state drift, process loss of control, data contamination, cross-turn inducement, and cascading
errors. These risks are often harder to detect and more difficult to cover with static testing.
Such risks typically exhibit three characteristics. First, they often emerge in long-horizon,
multi-step interactions rather than single-turn input-output pairs. Second, they are closely
tied to environmental states, tool interfaces,

Such risks typically exhibit three characteristics. First, they often emerge in long-horizon,
multi-step interactions rather than single-turn input-output pairs. Second, they are closely
tied to environmental states, tool interfaces, and execution contexts, making them difficult to
characterize using static benchmarks alone. Third, when they do occur, the consequences
often go beyond degraded response quality - they may further lead to erroneous operations,
privacy breaches, system damage, or even real-world losses. Therefore, trustworthy AI for
high-value scenarios requires not just "safer responses," but more importantly, the ability to
remain controllable, auditable, and recoverable while taking continuous action.
1.2
Limitations of Existing Systems
To address the above challenges, existing AI agent safety systems still exhibit significant
gaps across three foundational dimensions: evaluation, data, and evolution. These issues are
not isolated from one another. Collectively, they result in safety efforts often remaining in a
passive "find a problem, fix a problem" mode, making it difficult to form a continuous closed
loop oriented toward real-world deployment scenarios.
Limitations of Evaluation Infrastructure
Existing security evaluations are still dom-
inated by static question-answering, single-round adversarial examples, or fixed task sets.
Such approaches are suitable for answering whether a model "will say something wrong,"
but struggle to answer whether an agent "will do something wrong during continuous ex-
ecution." When risks depend on tool invocation chains, changes in environmental states,
and the accumulation of long-horizon decisions, single-turn interactive evaluations can only
capture the surface of the iceberg, making it difficult to systematically expose deep failure
modes. Even in executable environments, many existing frameworks remain centered around
fixed tasks and offline logs, lacking capabilities such as high-concurrency retesting, dynamic

Limitations of Evolution infrastructure
At the level of model evolution, training,
evaluation, and deployment remain largely disconnected from one another. Issues identified
during evaluation are difficult to promptly convert into training samples, and real-world
feedback generated during execution is also hard to smoothly reintegrate into subsequent
optimization workflows. Meanwhile, reinforcement learning and online distillation for agent
scenarios are transitioning from standalone algorithm implementations to system-level or-
chestration problems. However, most existing training frameworks are still designed around
local coordination between rollout and trainer components, and are insufficient to stably

support complex training closed loops that involve multi-environment concurrency, trajectory
staleness control, asynchronous sampling, and online policy updates. For a comprehensive
review, see Section 2.3.
1.3
Safactory: Building a Complete Closed Loop for Trustworthy
AI
To address the above limitations, Safactory proposes an infrastructure framework for trustwor-
thy autonomous agents. Centered around three core platforms - a Parallel Execution Middle
Platform, a Trustworthy Data Middle Platform, and an Autonomous Evolution Middle Plat-
form - it establishes a complete closed loop that integrates risk discovery, data accumulation,
and capability repair and reinforcement. Overall, as shown in Figure 1, Safactory does not
treat security evaluation as a one-time tool-based process, but rather organizes it as a data
and training system that can run continuously, be iteratively optimized, and be repeatedly
reused.
Among these, the Parallel Execution Middle Platform targets executable sandboxes for
Android, computer environments, and embodied scenarios. It supports environment pool
preheating, asynchronous scheduling, and concurrent re-evaluation of multiple trajectories, as
well as the injection of attacks and perturbations during execution, enabling more systematic
discovery of safety risks in long-horizon interactions

text:
supports environment pool
preheating, asynchronous scheduling, and concurrent re-evaluation of multiple trajectories, as
well as the injection of attacks and perturbations during execution, enabling more systematic
discovery of safety risks in long-horizon interactions. The Trustworthy Data Middle Platform
is responsible for consolidating the multimodal data, step-level trajectories, and behavioral
signals generated during evaluation and training into retrievable, analyzable, and auditable
data assets, and performs safety auditing, sample filtering, and experience distillation through
an intent-driven data engine. The Autonomous Evolution Middle Platform further integrates
these data assets into asynchronous reinforcement learning and online policy optimization
workflows, continuously generating trainable samples under version control, trajectory filtering,
and group consistency constraints, thereby supporting the simultaneous improvement of
model capabilities and safety.
Therefore, Safactory’s goal is neither to improve a single evaluation metric in isolation, nor
to merely provide point solutions such as a data tool or a training framework. What it aims
to establish is a closed-loop pathway of "discovering risks through execution, consolidating
evidence through data, completing repairs through evolution, and verifying effectiveness
through re-execution." Along this path, safety is no longer a one-time check conducted before
model deployment, but rather becomes an infrastructure capability throughout the entire
lifecycle of an agent.
Related Work
2.1
Agent Evaluation Framework
As model capabilities and deployment scenarios continue to expand, safety evaluation must
evolve toward deeper and more comprehensive paradigms. We therefore define a five-level
progression of AI safety evaluation (L1-L5):
Safactory: A Scalable Agent Factory for Trustworthy Autonomous Intelligence
Figure 3 The evolutionary trajectory of safety evaluation capabilities.
• L1 - Q&A Evaluation: Static, single-turn benchmarks focusing on alignment,
jailbreak robustness, and domain-specific knowledge.
• L2 - Adversarial Evaluation: Dynamic red teaming via human or automated
agents that generate multi-turn attack scenarios.
• L3 - Process-Level Evaluation: Multi-turn interaction assessment that captures
risks emerging task execution.
• L4 - Sandbox Evaluation: Evaluation in executable environments where real-world
consequences of agent actions are observable.
• L5 - Evolutionary Evaluation: Adaptive evaluation that co-evolves with model
capabilities and generalizes across scenarios.
2.1.1
Q&A - and Adversarial-Level Infrastructure
Most existing evaluation infrastructure operate at L1, organizing benchmarks around task
registries and

2.1.1
Q&A - and Adversarial-Level Infrastructure
Most existing evaluation infrastructure operate at L1, organizing benchmarks around task
registries and standardized scoring pipelines. Representative systems include LM-Evaluation-
Harness [30], OpenCompass [62], and OpenAI Evals [61]. Despite differences in scale and
engineering maturity, they share a common paradigm: evaluation is conducted on fixed,
single-instance tasks, inherently limiting coverage.
To overcome this limitation, L2 adversarial evaluation introduces dynamic attack generation.
Systems such as Giskard Hub [31], Garak [26], and promptfoo [69] expand the test space
through continuous red teaming and structured probing. However, their evaluation unit
remains single-turn interaction.
Both L1 and L2 fundamentally rely on single-turn interactions. In contrast, many critical
agent failures emerge over multi-step tool usage and long-horizon execution,
motivating the shift toward process-level evaluation.
Safactory: A Scalable Agent Factory for Trustworthy Autonomous Intelligence
2.1.2
Process-Level Infrastructure
L3 evaluation extends the unit of analysis from single responses to full interaction trajecto-
ries. Langfuse [47] emphasizes observability, enabling real-time monitoring of agent traces.
MLflow [57] integrates intermediate decision steps-such as tool selection and recovery-into
lifecycle evaluation. DeepEval [17] evolves from Q&A-style testing by introducing agentic
and conversation-level metrics.
Process-level evaluation enables trajectory-based analysis of where and how risks arise.
However, its scope largely remains within abstract task execution, lacking direct validation
of real-world consequences.
2.1.3
Sandbox-Level Infrastructure
Many agent risks-such as unsafe tool usage, improper file operations, and cascading execution
errors-cannot be fully captured without executable environments. L4 sandbox evaluation
addresses this by placing agents in real environments where outcomes are directly observable.
Systems such as Inspect AI [84], Terminal-Bench [55], Harbor [77], and Claw-Eval [95] advance
this paradigm across tool use, terminal tasks, and trajectory auditing.
However, such systems generally operate with fixed evaluation tasks as their basic unit, lacking
the ability to dynamically perturb the environment state, and store results primarily as logs
rather than reusable structured data. Environment support is often domain-specific, and
safety is treated as a secondary metric rather than

the ability to dynamically perturb the environment state, and store results primarily as logs
rather than reusable structured data. Environment support is often domain-specific, and

To address these gaps, Safactory introduces three key capabilities: (1) dynamic injection
of attacks and perturbations during execution to systematically expose failure modes; (2)
structured storage of interaction trajectories and reward signals as reusable data assets for
downstream SFT or RL; and (3) service-oriented environment interfaces that decouple evalu-
ation from execution, enabling seamless integration of diverse environments (e.g., industrial
systems, simulators, embodied settings).
Unlike prior L4 systems, The core distinction of Safactory is not whether it supports execution
in real environments, but rather its transformation of security evaluation from a one-time
tool-based process into a sustainably evolving infrastructure capability.
2.2
Trustworthy Data Services
As the training scale of AI systems and agentic application scenarios continue to expand,
the data service infrastructure that underpins their operation must also evolve from static
pipelines toward orchestrable, perception-aware, and self-evolving intelligent systems. We
categorize existing work into three stages according to capability progression:
• Static Data Management: Offline workflow scheduling built around DAGs, ensuring
data availability and reproducibility.
• Data Cleaning Pipelines: Orchestrable pipelines centered on rules, scoring functions,
Safactory: A Scalable Agent Factory for Trustworthy Autonomous Intelligence
and operators, automating collection, cleaning, deduplication, filtering, alignment, and
quality verification to reliably produce training-ready data.
• Intelligent Data Engines: Systems centered on data assets and behavioral signals,
transforming production-side feedback into a continuous optimization driver and forming
a closed-loop evolutionary cycle.
2.2.1
Static Data Management: DAG-centric Workflow Scheduling
Most mainstream data infrastructure revolves around task definition, dependency orches-
tration, scheduling, and status monitoring, with DAG-based batch processing at the core.
Apache Airflow [3] is the de facto standard for scheduling-oriented ETL, providing a DAG-
based framework with a rich operator library and cron scheduling. Prefect [68] advances
the paradigm from DAG as configuration to Python as flow, offering dynamic process con-
struction and event-driven scheduling. Dagster [19] places software-defined assets at
title: 2605.06230v1

text:
based framework with a rich operator library and cron scheduling. Prefect [68] advances
the paradigm from DAG as configuration to Python as flow, offering dynamic process con-
struction and event-driven scheduling. Dagster [19] places software-defined assets at the core,
treating datasets, models, and features as traceable products. All three share the same model:
execution is driven by static scheduling and predefined dependencies.
The limitation is that DAGs only answer when to run what, not whether the resulting data
is worth using. Dirty data, duplicate samples, and low-quality content can pass through
unchecked. This gap gave rise to quality-oriented data cleaning pipelines.
2.2.2
Data Cleaning Pipelines: Scoring-centric Quality Assurance
Data cleaning pipelines treat data quality as the primary objective, building workflows around
operators for cleaning, deduplication, filtering, and scoring. Data-Juicer [13] provides compos-
able operators covering text, image, audio/video, and multimodal data. OpenDataArena [9]
characterizes instructions and responses along more than ten dimensions, making data
value quantifiable. NeMo Curator [59] uses a GPU-accelerated stack to handle cleaning,
deduplication, and PII redaction with dedicated multimodal pipelines.
These systems shift from task scheduling to quality-centered content selection. However,
processing flows and scoring thresholds are fixed before the pipeline runs. Data risks surface
only when task objectives change, domains shift, or compliance boundaries tighten, which is
a structural limitation that static pipelines cannot overcome.
2.2.3
Intelligent Data Engines: Closed-loop Optimization Centered on Data
Assets and Behavioral Signals
Intelligent data engines treat data as continuously accumulable assets and use behavioral
signals as the core driver of data production. TensorZero [81] unifies an LLM gateway,
evaluation, and optimization into one system, persisting each inference and feedback while
supporting SFT and RLHF based on real-world metrics. Arena Learning [53] simulates a
chatbot arena to automatically generate preference pairs. Agent-in-the-Loop [98] embeds
human annotation into production workflows. Both validate the closed loop of "behavioral
signals →data assets →model iteration".

These systems, however, remain at the tool execution layer. Users must still describe execution
flows via declarative configurations, feedback is only aggregated as training signals rather
than used to evolve

actory: A Scalable Agent Factory for Trustworthy Autonomous Intelligence
These systems, however, remain at the tool execution layer. Users must still describe execution
flows via declarative configurations, feedback is only aggregated as training signals rather
than used to evolve the toolchain, and data security relies on environment isolation rather
than native substrate capabilities. In regulated and multi-party scenarios, this means task
expression is locked within engineering teams, closed-loop experience cannot be distilled into
reusable capabilities, and privacy constraints render many high-value tasks infeasible.
Safactory models data tasks as a complete chain: natural language intent →dynamically
generated pipeline →tool invocation →execution →tracking →asset accumulation. For
explicit tasks it uses single-planning execution. For ambiguous tasks it enters autonomous
closed-loop mode, continuously trying, judging, and correcting around the goal. Stable
multi-step combinations can be distilled into new tools, continuously evolving the tool
ecosystem. For data security, Safactory supports encrypted packaging for local execution,
outputting results without exposing raw data. This transforms data services from static
pipeline construction to intent-driven continuous evolution, which is the essential difference
between existing intelligent data engines and Safactory.
2.3
Agentic Model Training Services
With the expansion of large models into new application domains, training is no longer just
about optimizing model parameters. It now encompasses organizing the rollout process,
feedback modeling, and policy updates within the training pipeline. Traditional monolithic
training frameworks, which are designed for static data, are increasingly inadequate to support
more complex training processes. Consequently, model training services for agents have
gradually evolved into frameworks that emphasize orchestration, decoupling, and scalability.
Early reinforcement learning training frameworks for large language models (LLMs) mainly
focused on the standard Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF) process, as
exemplified by systems such as TRL[38], DeepSpeed-Chat[94], and OpenRLHF[63]. These
frameworks aimed to organize supervised fine-tuning, reward modeling, and policy optimiza-
tion into reproducible training pipelines. The training paradigm in this phase was relatively
standardized, typically following the SFT-RM-PPO (Supervised Fine-Tuning, Reward Model-
ing, Proximal Policy Optimization) pipeline: first, the model was aligned using demonstration
or preference data, then further optimized through reinforcement learning with a reward
model and PPO-based methods. This approach closely mirrored earlier representative

, Reward Model-
ing, Proximal Policy Optimization) pipeline: first, the model was aligned using demonstration
or preference data, then further optimized through reinforcement learning with a reward
model and PPO-based methods. This approach closely mirrored earlier representative works
such as InstructGPT[64] and Constitutional AI[5]. Thus, the core task during this phase was
the engineering and encapsulation of standard components like SFT, RM, and PPO, rather
than explicitly addressing multi-round interactive training in more complex environments.
As model scale, training costs, and online sampling expenses continued to rise, the focus
of training frameworks shifted from simply "executing RLHF" to "organizing large-scale
post-training systems." From GPT-3[8] and GPT-4[60] to Qwen2[70], DeepSeek-V2[21], and
DeepSeek-V3[22], these works demonstrated that model training is evolving into a multi-stage,
systematized engineering process: model improvement no longer solely depends on a single
training script, but increasingly relies on reusable data pipelines, clearly defined training
phases, and parallelism and scheduling mechanisms that scale stably with both model and
Safactory: A Scalable Agent Factory for Trustworthy Autonomous Intelligence
cluster sizes. At the framework level, verl[86] has begun to prioritize data flow organization,
modular decoupling, and resource scheduling, with a focus that has gradually shifted from
supporting a specific optimization method to providing unified system support for complex
post-training processes such as PPO and DPO.
In recent years, the rapid development of reasoning and agent-based scenarios has further
accelerated this evolution. While early RLHF efforts primarily addressed "how to make
models answer better," recent technical reports have focused more on "how to enable models
to act continuously in complex environments and learn from interactions." On the one hand,
works such as DeepSeek-R1[23] and Kimi k1.5[58] show that reinforcement learning has shifted
from being a tool for alignment to becoming a key method for enhancing reasoning capabilities.
Corresponding optimization methods have moved beyond the classic RLHF-PPO pipeline,
adopting new approaches such as GRPO that are better suited to large-scale online sampling
and relative comparison signals. On the other hand, reports such as Qwen3[71], GLM-
4.5[32], Kimi K2[45], Tongyi

and relative comparison signals. On the other hand, reports such as Qwen3[71], GLM-

Against this backdrop, training frameworks are transitioning from execution frameworks
focused on a single training cycle to systematic orchestration frameworks designed for
complex agent workflows. Works such as slime[82], AReaL[40, 29], and RollArt[73] explore
scalable organizational methods for more complex training workflows, from customizable
data generation interfaces and asynchronous training execution to decoupling heterogeneous
infrastructures. These frameworks share the characteristic of no longer being restricted to
implementing specific optimization algorithms like PPO or GRPO; instead, they focus on
system-wide coordination among rollout, reasoning, training, and resource orchestration.
Along this path, the core challenge in large model training services has shifted from "how
to complete a single training run" to "how to organize a sustainable and scalable training
system."
Compared to previous frameworks that were mainly designed around the collaboration
between rollout and trainer, Safactory’s advantage lies not in introducing new single-point
optimization modules, but in explicitly reconfiguring the structural relationships between the
operational pipeline, data deposition, and optimization loops through layered orchestration.
This makes the system more suited to handling the training requirements of agent-based
scenarios, where continuous interaction, generation, and optimization co-exist.
Parallel Simulation Platform
As agents extend toward multi-step, complex tasks, continuous-interaction evaluation in
real-world environments faces two core challenges. The first is long runtime and low efficiency.
Long decision chains make even minor deviations highly prone to accumulation, so repeated
trials are required to ensure confidence. Taking OpenClaw as an example, a single task takes
Safactory: A Scalable Agent Factory for Trustworthy Autonomous Intelligence
about 20 minutes on average. If 100 tasks are each re
title: 2605.06230v1

text:
trials are required to ensure confidence. Taking OpenClaw as an example, a single task takes
Safactory: A Scalable Agent Factory for Trustworthy Autonomous Intelligence
about 20 minutes on average. If 100 tasks are each re-evaluated five times, the total serial
runtime would be roughly seven days. The second challenge is insufficient assessment of
safety capabilities. The current evaluation paradigm, based on single-sample, linear execution,
fundamentally conflicts with the exponential growth of multi-turn interactive decision branches
in multi-step tasks. In OpenClaw, for instance, an agent may trigger high-risk operations such
as accidentally deleting files during task execution. However, single-path testing can cover
only a very small number of fixed trajectories, making it easy to create an illusion of safety
simply because the model happened to avoid risk in one particular decoding attempt. Since
existing evaluations lack mechanisms for traversing decision branches in multi-step tasks,
they struggle to systematically expose risk blind spots in complex interactions, resulting in
inadequate safety-evaluation coverage and distorted conclusions.
To systematically address these pain points, the Parallel Rollout Platform was developed. As
a sandbox rollout framework designed for evaluation and reinforcement learning, the platform
uses pre-warmed environment pools and asynchronous scheduling mechanisms to advance
massive numbers of long-horizon trajectories under high concurrency, effectively alleviating
efficiency and cost bottlenecks. At the same time, it natively supports the dynamic injection
of red-team attacks, enabling comprehensive and systematic evaluation of agents’ safety and
robustness boundaries in complex interactive scenarios.
In addition, the platform continuously accumulates reusable data and experience during
operation. All step-level trajectories generated through rollouts can be seamlessly fed back
into the system, forming a closed data loop of "evaluation-training-re-evaluation." In this
way, the Parallel Rollout Platform fundamentally breaks through the limitations of isolated
evaluation and establishes a unified technical foundation for the continuous improvement of
agent capabilities and the strengthening of safety defenses.

3.2
System Architecture
Figure 4 Architecture of Parallel Simulation Platform.
To support multi-step continuous-interaction evaluation and address issues such as long
evaluation time and insufficient evaluation coverage, we designed and implemented a parallel
rollout platform based on state awareness and elastic scheduling (Figure 4). This platform is
designed for the scaled execution of evaluation tasks and multi-path analysis. It centrally
manages Agent instances, sandbox environments, large-model requests, and the circulation of

improves evaluation throughput through an end-to-end mechanism of "pre-warming-pooled
scheduling-asynchronous persistence." First, the sandbox manager starts the underlying
sandboxes in batches in advance according to the configured pool capacity, completing
the pre-warming of the task execution resource pool and reducing cold-start time from

between tasks through asynchronous hot-switching scheduling. This reduces CPU idle time
caused by Agent initialization, environment loading, and resource waiting, allowing limited
computing resources to be used more for the actual evaluation process. Finally, in the face
of request surges to large models caused by repeated evaluation with multiple Agents, the
elastic large-model gateway is responsible for unified handling, dynamic scaling, and request
forwarding, and transparently writes interaction trajectories into the storage module. With
built-in buffering and a "fire-and-forget" asynchronous persistence mechanism, the main
evaluation process does not need to block while waiting for trajectory writing. In this way,
repeated evaluation of long tasks that was originally advanced serially is transformed into a
multi-instance concurrent execution process, significantly reducing overall evaluation time
while ensuring that trajectories remain traceable.
To address problems in safety evaluation such as insufficient coverage under single-path
execution, high costs of repeated restarts, and difficulty reproducing abnormal scenarios,
the platform further introduces a "backtracking sandbox" mechanism, building a version
management system similar to a Git dedicated to Agents. This mechanism can perceive
file changes, context states, and key environment operations made by the Agent during
the task process, and automatically generate checkpoints at important nodes. Like game
saves, it preserves a complete behavior timeline and an environment "version tree." During
evaluation, when an Agent enters a multi-path decision node, triggers a potentially high-risk
operation, or causes environment damage due to incorrect execution, the system does not
need to fully restart from the initial state. Instead, it can precisely locate a historical
safety anchor point and quickly roll back to the corresponding state, then continue rolling
out different action paths as if "pulling a new branch." As a result, safety evaluation is
upgraded from single-sample, linear execution to branch-based exploration around key states.

3.3
Supported Agent Environments and Experiments
In this section, we present the diverse agent environments supported by Safactory and their
corresponding evaluation methodologies. As illustrated in Figure 5, the framework is built
around a unified interaction protocol that standardizes the integration of heterogeneous
environments, including QA, mobile (Android), desktop (OS), embodied interaction, and
tool-use scenarios. By coupling a consistent simulation pipeline with trajectory logging and
metric aggregation, Safactory enables unified evaluation across environments.
Moreover, the framework supports the injection of various risk-oriented attacks during rollout,
allowing systematic assessment of model capability, safety, and robustness under different
conditions. Based on this unified infrastructure, we conduct experiments across QA, Android,
OS, Embodied, and OpenClaw environments. The comparison models include claude-opus-4-
6-thinking, qwen3.5-plus, kimi-k2.5, glm-4.6v, and step-3; in text-based benchmarks, we also
evaluated models such as MiniMax-M2.5, DeepSeek-V3.2, and step-3.5-flash.

As shown in Figure 6, the safety performance of different models in this environment exhibits
a clear stratification. Leading models demonstrate relatively stable resistance to attacks,

The evaluation tasks are built upon OSWorld [90] and RiOSWorld [42], covering desktop
operation workflows and security risk scenarios respectively. In security evaluation mode,
the system not only assesses task completion but also tracks risk-triggering behaviors, and
computes a comprehensive performance metric based on both. The reward design is directly
derived from the final task score: binary rewards in standard mode, and multi-level discrete
rewards in security mode, which are used to distinguish different combinations of task
completion and risk triggers.
As shown in Figure 7, the evaluated models fall into two distinct categories. One group
adopts a more aggressive strategy in advancing tasks, achieving higher completion rates but
also exhibiting a greater tendency to cross risk boundaries. The other group demonstrates
stronger avoidance behavior, achieving higher safety at the cost of reduced task progression
efficiency. Only a few models approach a balanced trade-off between the two.
This indicates that risks in desktop environments arise not only from local interface mis-
interpretation, but also from the accumulation of state uncertainty across long chains of
cross-application interactions. Consequently, OS scenarios are more effective than mobile
settings in exposing the core challenge of GUI agents: models must continuously make
fine-grained trade-offs between proactive action and risk avoidance.

The evaluation is built upon GhostEI-Bench [12], covering a variety of real-world mobile
applications and environment-injection attack scenarios, including static injection, dynamic
prompting, and misleading instructions. The core metrics include task success rate and attack
exposure-related indicators, capturing capability and safety performance, respectively. The
reward is defined in a terminal, binary manner: a positive reward is assigned only when the
agent successfully completes the task without triggering any attack behavior; otherwise, the
reward is zero. This design avoids biases introduced by process-level reward shaping and
ensures alignment between training objectives and final evaluation metrics.
As shown in Figure 8, capability and safety in the Android environment do not exhibit a
clear trade-off; instead, the distribution suggests a positive correlation. Models with stronger
task completion ability are generally less susceptible to interface manipulation or malicious
injection. This indicates that, in mobile GUI scenarios, stronger capabilities in interface
understanding, goal recognition, and action selection naturally contribute to improved safety.

Embodied Gym
Safactory supports embodied scenarios through both RoboTrust Gym and Embodied Gym.
The former targets household embodied interaction tasks, focusing on evaluating execution
stability and safety robustness under dynamic disturbances and uncertainty. The latter is
built upon ALFRED tasks, providing a multi-stage embodied interaction environment in
indoor 3D scenes, and emphasizing the agent’s ability to follow natural language instructions
to perform sequential actions such as goal finding, object manipulation, and state completion.
These two environments correspond to two complementary questions: "Can the agent act
safely?" and "Can the agent accomplish tasks effectively?"
At the execution level, RoboTrust Gym is built on the Habitat simulation environment,
while Embodied Gym relies on the AI2-THOR stack and EmbodiedBench [92]. Both involve
graphical rendering and physical interaction simulation, leading to relatively heavy system
dependencies. To reduce deployment overhead, we provide pre-configured environment images
to enable rapid setup and large-scale experimentation.

3.4
SATraj: Open-Source Agent Trajectory Datasets
Safactory is not only an execution framework for agent evaluation and training, but also
capable of continuously depositing agent interaction processes into data assets. During
each environmental operation, the framework automatically records the complete interaction
trajectories between agents and the environment, including task instructions, dialogue history,
model responses, action execution, environmental states, screenshot observations, stepwise
rewards, and final task outcomes. Consequently, each evaluation or training session generates
not only performance metrics but also high-quality trace data that is traceable, analyzable,
and reusable.
This mechanism extends Safactory from a single-round experimental platform to an infrastruc-
ture for continuous data asset accumulation. For complex agent tasks such as Computer-Using
Agents, real interaction trajectories are of great value. They can be utilized for behavioral im-
itation in supervised fine-tuning, provide rollout data for reinforcement learning, and further
support failure case analysis, experience extraction, safety alignment, and capability boundary
diagnosis. Equipped with a unified data management module, Safactory structurally persists
trajectories generated across diverse environments, models, and tasks, significantly reducing
the cost of data sorting and reuse after multiple rounds of experiments.
Taking OSGym as an example, Safactory can mass-produce agent trajectories in desktop
operating environments and solidify these trajectories into open datasets. Based on OSGym,
we have constructed and open-sourced the SATraj-OS1 dataset12. Oriented toward the
capability learning and safety training of Computer-Using Agents, this dataset contains
10,496 GUI interaction trajectories and 158,196 interaction steps, with an average trajectory
length of 15.07 steps. The dataset covers both capability-oriented and safety-oriented tasks.
Capability-related trajectories encompass scenarios involving browsers, GIMP, LibreOffice,
Thunderbird, VLC, VS Code, system operations, and multi-application collaboration. Safety-
related trajectories cover high-risk scenarios such as phishing webpages, pop-up inducements,
malicious scripts, unknown files, personal privacy information, GitHub submissions, Reddit

related trajectories cover high-risk scenarios such as phishing webpages, pop-up inducements,
malicious scripts, unknown files, personal privacy information, GitHub submissions, Reddit
operations, and account management behaviors.
Beyond environment execution and model evaluation, Safactory transforms agent operational
processes into a self-reinforcing, growing data flywheel. Agents interact within simulated
environments, and all interactions are automatically recorded as trajectories. After cleansing
and annotation, these trajectories are refined into training data and experience repositories,

Trustworthy Data Platform
4.1
Quick Start
The Trustworthy Data Platform adopts a dual-layer architecture comprising a data infras-
tructure layer and an intelligent data execution engine. The Data Infrastructure Layer
is responsible for establishing global data ingestion specifications and schema standards,
enabling unified management and assetization of PB-scale heterogeneous data. Built upon
this standardized foundation, the DataElf Engine executes complex inspection, cleaning, and
analysis tasks through intent-driven orchestration.
What is DataElf?
DataElf is an intelligent execution engine designed for large-scale data tasks.
Its core
value lies in "Intent Interpretation": users define objectives in natural language, and the
system automatically handles task decomposition, pipeline generation, tool invocation, and
result auditing. Furthermore, DataElf overcomes the performance bottlenecks of traditional
privacy-preserving computation by supporting controlled execution within local "black boxes,"
ensuring that data is "usable but invisible."
Core Capabilities of DataElf
• Natural Language Driven: Orchestrates and executes dynamic pipelines based on
simple natural language task descriptions.
• Evolutionary Pilot Mode: Continuously attempts, corrects, and autonomously
derives new tools for ambiguous tasks, which are then persisted as system assets.
• Trustworthy Execution Foundation: Operates within controlled sandboxes, pro-
viding cost-aware execution strategies (token/time budget control) and comprehensive

4.2
System Architecture
The core design of the system follows a triple logic of "decoupled storage and computing,
intelligent governance closed-loop, and compliance with trustworthiness," aiming to construct
an intelligent hub that supports the full-lifecycle management of PB-scale security data
assets.
The underlying architecture relies on the LanceDB Serverless system to build a high-
performance multi-modal data lakehouse, addressing the storage challenges of heterogeneous
and unstructured data generated by the security wind tunnel system during massive eval-
uation processes. This storage foundation achieves the decoupling of the Persistent layer,
Object Storage, and multi-dimensional indexing layers through a tiered design. It not only
supports the reliable storage and computation of ultra-large-scale data but also ensures
low-latency data access via a self-developed Multi-layer Cache mechanism. This enables
the platform to support a spectrum of queries ranging from simple business tag filtering to
complex semantic-level similarity recall, providing precise, versioned, and reproducible data

4.3

4.3.1
Data Value Assessment Tool
The data value assessment tool offers an end-to-end pipeline for judging the training value of a
dataset and distilling it into a high-quality subset. It comprises two complementary stages: a
scoring stage that assigns quality scores through pluggable scorers, and a selection stage that
turns these scores into a diverse, high-value subset of the requested size. Although designed
to work together, the two stages are fully decoupled and can also be invoked independently:
users may run scoring alone to obtain multi-dimensional quality signals for their downstream
workflows, or run selection alone on top of pre-computed scores.
Scoring. The scoring stage assigns a quality score to every sample in a dataset. It supports
pluggable scoring methods: users can switch between scorers or run multiple scorers on the
same dataset to obtain multi-dimensional quality signals.
The platform currently integrates 9 scorers that cover complementary quality dimensions [10].
PPL [41] and Norm Loss [78] measure the target model’s predictability of a training sample
from a probabilistic and information-compression perspective: the more natural and fluent a
passage appears to the model, the higher the score, whereas a low score indicates that the
sample is unnatural to the model. IFD [49] compares the relative difficulty of generating
the same response with and without its instruction, using the gap as a proxy for how
much the instruction actually helps: the more it helps, the higher the quality.
DEITA Q
and DEITA C [52] rate the intrinsic properties of a sample directly: the former focuses on
whether the instruction-response pair is clear and accurate, while the latter independently
evaluates instruction difficulty and complexity. DeBERTa [37] applies a classifier trained on
human-labelled data to score coherence and grammatical accuracy, while FineWeb Edu [65]
focuses on educational value such as structured explanation. Ask LLM [74] adopts the most
direct approach, simply prompting an off-the-shelf large language model to judge whether
the sample is high-quality. Finally, we propose a composite strategy named DataElf as the
platform’s default scorer: it converts the IFD and DEITA Q scores into percentile ranks and
fuses them with equal weight, yielding a single score that jointly reflects instruction-response
clarity, accuracy, and reasoning difficulty.

On the engineering side, every scorer runs independently per sample and can fully exploit GPU

Selection. Given the quality scores produced above, the selection stage draws a target
number of high-value samples from the dataset. Naively taking the top-K samples by score
tends to produce a homogeneous subset that lacks diversity in topic and difficulty. To mitigate
this, the tool first encodes each sample into a semantic vector with an embedding model
( Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct ), applies K-means clustering, allocates the overall selection quota
across clusters in proportion to their sizes, and finally picks the highest-scoring samples
within each cluster. This simple yet effective strategy preserves data diversity while keeping
the selected subset locally high-quality within every cluster.

4.3.2
Scientific Data Processing Tool
With the continued growth of scientific research data, efficiently acquiring and processing
domain-specific data has become a key challenge in building AI4Science pipelines. Unlike
general-purpose text or structured business data, scientific data sources are highly fragmented:
protein sequences, EEG signals, climate observations, and molecular structures are stored
in domain-specific databases and file formats, lacking unified access protocols and data
standards. At the same time, the data processing tool ecosystems across domains remain
isolated, with heterogeneous output formats and interfaces that are difficult to compose into
a unified AI analysis pipeline.
The scientific data processing toolkit (SciTools) is a domain-specific tool layer within the
DataElf tool ecosystem for scientific computing scenarios. Its tool taxonomy follows the
domain division idea proposed by SciDataCopilot[72], and currently covers four domains:
bioinformatics (bio), neural signals (eeg), earth science (earth), and chemistry (chem).
It currently includes five operators: the cross-database enzyme property retrieval tool
enzyme acquire, the protein sequence physicochemical analysis tool protein analyzer,
an EEG signal processing tool, a climate data alignment tool, and a molecular structure
standardization tool, built on mainstream domain libraries including UniProt[85], KEGG[43],

The data security audit tool is designed to detect security risks across multiple dimensions in
post-training datasets, helping practitioners identify and mitigate harmful content, privacy
leakage, and adversarial attacks before data enters the training pipeline. Built around a
pluggable checker architecture, the tool ships with 23 checkers spanning 13 risk types: harmful

approach.
Rule-based checkers perform deterministic matching via regular expressions and keyword
lists, running offline without any model.
PIIRule detects phone numbers, emails, ID

Model-based checkers use task-specific trained models for classification. BiasClassifier

to extract structured privacy entities. JailbreakClassifier uses WildGuard [34] to detect

Heuristic checkers consist of GraCeFulBackdoorDefender , which implements the gradient-
based analysis method from GraCeFul [88] to detect backdoor-poisoned samples through
statistical clustering across the full dataset. Unlike the preceding three categories, this checker
must process the entire dataset in batch mode to extract dataset-level statistical patterns.
Each risk type carries a configurable weight, and the final safety score is derived from a
weighted penalty formula, where higher scores indicate cleaner datasets.
All non-heuristic checkers operate independently at the sample level and can be parallelized
across multiple threads. Heuristic checkers execute sequentially in batch mode once the
parallel phase finishes. After auditing, the tool generates a Markdown report covering risk
distribution statistics and per-sample details. Checkers are registered through a decorator
pattern, allowing users to mix, extend, or replace checkers via configuration.

4.3.4
Trajectory Analysis Tool
The trajectory analysis tool focuses on experience distillation from the agent’s step-level
interaction logs. Its goal is not simply to retain the original long trajectories, but to transform
them into retrievable, reusable, and evolvable skill or experience units. The overall design, on
one hand, draws inspiration from the skill file organization approach of "lightweight indexing
+ on-demand loading" in Claude Agent Skills [2]. On the other hand, it incorporates the
idea of "discovering high-level policies from historical trajectories and continuously evolving
them" proposed in works such as SkillRL [89], MemSkill [97], and EvoSkill [1]. Meanwhile,
SkillsBench also demonstrates that highly curated and well-organized skills can stably enhance
agent performance, although the quality control of automatically generated skills remains a
critical challenge [50].
Experiments
We evaluate the effectiveness of experience injection facilitated by the trajectory analysis
tool on the RiOSWorld benchmark. All baseline models execute tasks directly within the
OS environment without utilizing additional skills. The evaluation metrics include the
completion rate and the safety rate, where the safety rate represents the proportion of
task executions that do not trigger any risks. The test tasks cover subcategories such as
induced text , phishing web , popup , libre office , multimedia , note attack , os ,
python script , and reddit .
Based on this, we select kimi-k2.5 as the base model and inject the skills distilled from
historical interactions by the trajectory analysis tool into the agent prompt to observe the
changes in overall safety. As shown in Figure 17, the results indicate that after utilizing
the skills, the overall safety rate of kimi-k2.5 increases from 32.17% to 71.08%, with the
corresponding risk trigger rate decreasing from 67.83% to 28.92%. This result demonstrates
that the lightweight experiences distilled by the trajectory analysis tool can significantly
reduce risk exposure in OS scenarios without modifying the model parameters. Currently, we
have only completed the verification of the overall safety rate; the remaining sub-category
results will be supplemented subsequently.

Autonomous Evolution Platform
The autonomous evolution platform focuses on the "continuous enhancement of model
capabilities." It centers around the RL training loop, organizing sample generation and
policy updates into an iterative feedback loop: one end continuously integrates environmental
feedback and multi-source trajectories, while the other end employs the OPD mechanism
to convert usable signals from the teacher model into stable updates. A unified metric
system tracks both gains and degradations. The core value of this system lies in its ability to
support large-scale asynchronous training tasks: even under conditions with complex data
sources and frequent configuration changes, it can consistently perform sample production,
assembly, and policy updates, while ensuring the training process is observable, controllable,
and reproducible.

5.2
Agentic RL System Architecture
Figure 18 Agentic RL System Architecture
The Agentic RL system architecture, as shown in Figure 18, is designed around three core
modules: Bridge, Trajectory Transformation, and Buffer Server. These modules jointly
support a fully asynchronous "Sampling-Trajectory Transformation-Training" pipeline. The
architecture separates the rollout and training processes and uses versioned metadata and
group consistency mechanisms to ensure that the system can reliably generate high-quality
training samples under complex, long-duration, multi-environment concurrent tasks.
5.2.1
Bridge Module: Asynchronous Control and Unified Inference Entry
In traditional reinforcement learning architectures, interactions with the environment, infer-
ence requests, and training consumption are often tightly coupled, limiting system scalability
and flexibility. In a fully asynchronous setup, issues such as policy version misalignment with
sampled data, missing sample groups, trajectory and supervision signal misalignment, and
imbalanced production-consumption in data buffers can arise. To address this, the system in-
troduces the LLM Proxy and Rollout Manager at the Bridge layer, forming a closed loop with
the Buffer Server: The LLM Proxy writes and returns trajectory and model version metadata
in the request path, enabling the training endpoint to perform data staleness filtering and
mitigate policy lag. The Rollout Manager samples by instance/group and submits in groups
to prevent incomplete sample groups from entering training. The Bridge unifies the routing
Safactory: A Scalable Agent Factory for Trustworthy Autonomous Intelligence
of LLM sessions and links trajectory recording and transformation processes, reducing the
chances of trajectory and supervision signal mismatch. The Buffer Server dequeues data as
needed, balancing production and consumption rates to prevent queue buildup and starvation.
As a result, Rollout and Train are decoupled temporally, while maintaining consistency in
data semantics and quality constraints.

At the same time, the Bridge supports the use of the OPD (Online Policy Distillation)

works in collaboration with submodules for teacher model policy generation and other training

5.2.2
Alignment Module: Semantic Alignment from Trajectories to Trainable
Samples
In the Agentic RL system, trajectory data generated by the environment cannot be directly
used for training. It needs to go through a series of processing and transform steps to become
suitable training samples. The Trajectory Transformation module is designed to address this
issue. Through a fine-grained processing pipeline, it converts raw trajectories generated from
multi-round interactions into samples that meet training requirements.
The design of the Trajectory Transformation module focuses on ensuring data semantic consis-
tency and correctness. Through incremental encoding and prefix matching of trajectories, the
system effectively avoids redundant encoding of historical context while handling multimodal
inputs (e.g., image and text data). Additionally, the system ensures that losses are only
computed within the areas generated by the model through Mask Painter and Sample Packer,
ensuring effective control of data quality during the training process. Ultimately, the samples
are packed and annotated with metadata to maintain consistency in batch processing and
prevent training bias due to data issues.
5.2.3
Summary
The Agentic RL system achieves asynchronous orchestration through the Bridge module,
maintains data lifecycle consistency, ensures semantic correctness of data through the Trajec-
tory Transformation module, and guarantees data supply through the Buffer Server. Together,
these modules enable the system to maintain the usability, timeliness, and statistical stability
of training data under fully asynchronous conditions, successfully supporting unified training
for various task types.
5.3
On-policy Distillation
In the unified architecture above, OPD (On-policy Distillation) is implemented as an optional
training route rather than a prerequisite for the system’s establishment. Its primary role is
to provide additional distribution constraints and teacher signal correction for current policy
updates under asynchronous rollouts and long-range agent interactions, thereby enhancing
the consistency, stability, and controllability of the training process

to provide additional distribution constraints and teacher signal correction for current policy
updates under asynchronous rollouts and long-range agent interactions, thereby enhancing
the consistency, stability, and controllability of the training process. Recent reviews of
Safactory: A Scalable Agent Factory for Trustworthy Autonomous Intelligence
OPD also highlight that its core idea is to distill from the student’s own accessed state and
trajectory distributions, rather than only performing offline imitation from static teacher
data. Accordingly, OPD is particularly suited for training scenarios with more apparent
asynchronous sampling, policy delays, and distribution shifts. [91, 93]
From a system implementation perspective, OPD integrates into the existing five-layer
architecture as a plugin, without altering the main execution flow of Safactory. The teacher
bridge, teacher model ref, and OPD signal/granularity hook in the figure should be
understood as optional extension points: The teacher bridge in the proxy layer integrates
teacher information during request or response phases; the hook in the rollout engine aligns
distillation signals with the raw trajectories, and the teacher model ref at the bottom
provides an optional teacher strategy reference. In other words, OPD does not define the
core structure of the system but rather adds an additional supervisory interface on top of the
unified execution base.
In the current implementation, Safactory adopts a white-box, token-level form of OPD.
Specifically, the system introduces token-level log probabilities provided by the teacher during
the rollout phase and incorporates a reverse-KL regularization term into the training objective
to impose additional constraints on the advantage-driven policy updates.
From an architectural perspective, Safactory’s interface can further accommodate other forms
of teacher signals, such as step-level, trajectory-level, or higher granularity feedback. This
allows Safactory to maintain the system’s simplicity by using the same training base for
both Gym-style and agentic scenarios, while also reserving sufficient space for more complex
distillation, correction, and auxiliary supervision methods. [91]
OPD can be completely disabled in Safactory; however, for agentic tasks with more pronounced
issues of asynchronous sampling, long-range decision making, multi-round interactions, and
complex credit assignment, OPD can enhance the stability and controllability of the training
process without disrupting the main execution flow. This enables Safactory to support
both conventional reinforcement learning processes and naturally extend to more complex
autonomous evolution training parad

5.4.1
Safactory V.S. slime-native
The Safactory system supports both synchronous and asynchronous rollout execution modes.
In terms of architectural design, Safactory explicitly decouples the rollout and training
lifecycles: the data producer continuously generates trajectory data and writes it to storage,
while the training process is triggered by the Bridge module once a sufficient amount of
trainable trajectories has been accumulated.
This asynchronous mechanism effectively
prevents the "producer" from idling its computational resources, thereby improving overall
resource utilization.
In terms of data management, Safactory introduces a "trajectory staleness" mechanism
to filter samples in the buffer. Staleness is defined as the difference between the policy
version used for trajectory generation and the current policy version. For example, when the
Safactory: A Scalable Agent Factory for Trustworthy Autonomous Intelligence
acceptable staleness is set to 0, only trajectories generated by the current policy can be used
for training; when the acceptable staleness is set to 1, trajectories generated by both the
current and previous versions of the policy are allowed. This mechanism ensures training
stability while allowing developers to customize the combination of off-policy and on-policy
data, thereby increasing data reuse efficiency.

Safactory can support longer trajectory generation times while maintaining approximately

in tasks where the rollout computational cost dominates, the performance of both systems

Safactory is longer, generating more trajectories. Within a single step window, Slime-native
generates 512 trajectories, which matches the global batch size, while Safactory generates an
average of 1277.78 trajectories, which is 2.49 times the amount generated by Slime-native.
Further analysis revealed that, under the current experimental configuration, the rollout time
in Slime-native is shorter than the training time, leading to significant idling of inference-
side resources, with the idle proportion being around 50%. In contrast, Safactory’s fully
asynchronous architecture continuously generates trajectories during the training phase, thus
preventing GPU resource waste and achieving higher overall system throughput and resource
utilization efficiency.
DeepLink Computing Technology
As a domestic software-hardware collaborative open computing system for artificial intelligence,
DeepLink aims to build a bridge for adapting hardware chips and software frameworks, and
jointly foster an open software-hardware adaptation ecosystem[76]. Built on DeepLink, it
supports an integrated training and inference toolchain for domestic multi-chip heterogeneous
and cross-domain scenarios, fundamentally breaking ecological barriers, achieving software-
hardware decoupling, and diversifying computing power elements[75].
To empower the Safactory Framework to run in large-scale domestic environments, DeepLink
provides three core capabilities: Large-scale pooled inference, Distributed hierarchical data
storage, and Domestic heterogeneous training toolchain.

6.1
Large-scale pooled inference
Serving as the "Computing Hub" of the AI computing foundation, it provides the core
computing backbone for the risk inference middle platform. By building a fully domestic
inference cluster based on Ascend Atlas, it ensures business SLAs (Service Level Agreements)
while maximizing overall resource utilization. Through modeling and simulating the inference
process on heterogeneous hardware, it enables automated optimization of inference services,
achieving a cumulative 50% boost in throughput performance in typical large model scenarios.
6.2
Distributed hierarchical data storage
Serving as the "Data Cornerstone" of the AI computing foundation, it provides unified
storage for risk trajectory data generated by the inference middle platform, supporting diverse
formats including vectors, text, images, and videos. By leveraging hybrid retrieval and a
disaggregated compute-storage architecture, the system seamlessly integrates with object
storage and enables elastic scaling. This facilitates millisecond-level retrieval across PB-scale
massive samples and risk data.
6.3
Domestic heterogeneous training toolchain
As the "operational assurance" of the AI computing foundation, it provides a stable underlying
runtime environment for computing power scheduling and data processing. Based on Kunpeng
domestic chips, an integrated adaptation solution for training and sandbox runtime has been
developed. Through direct deployment via Linux containers and deep optimization using
Kernel Same-page Merging (KSM)[28], virtualization overhead and memory redundancy are
effectively reduced. This achieves a 50%+ boost in single-instance performance, an 80%+
reduction in memory usage, and a 6-fold increase in parallel scale, ensuring the efficient and
stable operation of the computing power base. Leveraging the Ascend Atlas 800 A3 series
super-node computing foundation, the system bridges the full-link process for training and
inference across heterogeneous cards. Through performance optimization methods such as
load balancing scheduling optimization and the One Step Off-Policy mechanism, it ensures
exceptional reliability and throughput efficiency for reinforcement learning workflows during
ultra-long-cycle operations.
Conclusions and Discussions

Safactory is motivated by a simple observation: once large models become autonomous agents,
safety can no longer be treated as a single benchmark score or a one-time release check.
Many important failures emerge only when agents interact with tools, environments, and
long decision chains. As a result, trustworthy agent development requires more than isolated
evaluation tools or standalone training frameworks. It requires an infrastructure that can
continuously discover risks, preserve evidence, convert execution traces into reusable assets,
and feed them back into model improvement.
In this report, we present Safactory as such an infrastructure. Its Parallel Simulation
Safactory: A Scalable Agent Factory for Trustworthy Autonomous Intelligence
Platform moves safety evaluation from static samples to large-scale sandbox-based rollout
and risk discovery. Its Trustworthy Data Platform turns trajectories, rewards, and
intermediate logs into structured, auditable, and retrievable data assets, while supporting
safety auditing and experience extraction. Its Autonomous Evolution Platform further
connects these assets to asynchronous reinforcement learning and online policy optimization,
making it possible to organize rollout, data transformation, and policy update in a unified
loop. Supported by DeepLink computing technologies, Safactory also provides the computing,
storage, and system foundations needed to run this loop at scale.
From this perspective, the main contribution of Safactory is not a single algorithm or
benchmark.
Rather, it is a system view of trustworthy autonomous intelligence: risk
discovery, data deposition, capability repair, and re-validation should be designed as one
continuous process. This design helps bridge the long-standing gap between evaluation, data,
and training, and provides a practical foundation for building safer and more capable agents
in OS, mobile, embodied, and other interactive environments.
At the same time, Safactory should be viewed as a starting point rather than a finished
solution. Several challenges remain open. First, environment coverage and scenario realism
still need to expand, especially for high-stakes domains with richer human, tool, and system
interactions. Second, automatically judging agent safety and utility in long-horizon tasks
remains difficult, and stronger metrics and oversight mechanisms are still needed. Third,
turning execution traces into stable training gains is still an evolving problem, especially
under asynchronous sampling, distribution shift, and complex credit assignment. We hope
Safactory can serve as a shared foundation for future work on scalable agent evaluation,
trustworthy data construction, and continual post-training for autonomous systems.

results highlight the advantage of DataElf over every other scorer.
